questions.question: You have planned and begun the current Sprint where the project team have committed to delivering and releasing a particular feature. You need to see how the team are tracking towards their Sprint goal. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the Kanban board
B. Review the team velocity chart
C. Review the Sprint burndown chart
D. Perform a Sprint review with the team
questions.explanation: A Sprint burndown chart is a graphical representation of the work remaining in a time box (iteration or Sprint) and compares the ideal trend with the items actually completed, making it the best tool to track progress towards the Sprint goal. A Kanban board shows work items but not burndown over time. A velocity chart measures capacity across Sprints rather than current Sprint progress. A Sprint review is performed after the Sprint is complete, not during it to track progress.

questions.question: Your agile project has recently been merged into a larger program and the program manager is reviewing how the project is tracking. She asks you how much has been completed in design, but also how much has been completed in development, testing, and sign-off. What will you do next?
options: A. Show her the Sprint Kanban board
B. Take her through the team product backlog
C. Show her the team cumulative flow diagram
D. Show her the team velocity chart
questions.explanation: A cumulative flow diagram indicates features completed over time separated at each stage (backlog, design, development, testing, sign-off, etc.), providing visibility into work distribution across all phases. A Kanban board shows only a single Sprint's work. A product backlog shows work items but not their phase distribution. A velocity chart measures capacity per Sprint, not work distribution across phases.

questions.question: The features developed by your agile team have added some complexity to your website, delivered quickly with no time to refactor the code so it is a bit messy. You believe the user stories are moving slower now as a result but you cannot be sure. What will you do next?
options: A. Check the cycle time chart of the team
B. Shadow the developers to overview the work they are doing
C. View the product roadmap for proposed release dates
D. Review the points in the Sprint backlog versus the last three Sprints velocity
questions.explanation: A cycle time chart shows the average cycle time of work items completed over time and can reveal if individual user stories are taking longer to complete due to code complexity. This directly measures how long it takes to complete each item from start to finish. Shadowing developers is not a data-driven approach. The product roadmap does not show item completion times. Comparing Sprint backlog points shows overall team velocity, not individual story cycle times.

questions.question: You are working on an agile project with a full product roadmap for the next 6 months. Your project team are not sure what the next feature does or how they should turn it into user stories. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the scope Baseline
B. Perform story mapping with your team
C. Perform Sprint planning with your team
D. Perform a retrospective with your team
questions.explanation: A story map is a visual model of all features and functionality desired for a given product, created to give the project team a holistic view of what they are building and why. It breaks features down into user stories based on functionality. This directly addresses the need to understand the feature and translate it into user stories. Sprint planning cannot occur until user stories are defined. A retrospective is held after a Sprint to improve processes, not to define requirements. A scope Baseline is more appropriate for traditional projects.

questions.question: You are a program manager on a large-scale agile program where each project team has its own Scrum Master. You need to know the rate at which work is flowing through each team so you can see if there are any blockers or issues and whether they will remain on track. What will you do next?
options: A. Raise your query at Scrum of Scrums
B. Attend the Sprint review of each team at the end of each Sprint
C. Review the schedule management plan for managing the schedule
D. Review the team velocity charts
questions.explanation: A velocity chart tracks the rate at which deliverables are produced, validated, and accepted within a predefined interval (Sprint) by showing what was committed versus what was completed. This metric directly shows the rate of work flowing through each team. Scrum of Scrums addresses coordination but not work rate metrics. Sprint reviews demonstrate completed work but do not quantify the rate of work flow. Schedule management plans describe processes, not current work rates.

questions.question: Your project has been executing for the past 5 months and due to an organizational change a new project sponsor has come on board. He asks where the project is up to and whether it is on track. You tell him but he wants more information. What will you do next?
options: A. Show him the signed project Charter
B. Show him the project management plan including the schedule management plan
C. Review the status report and previous status reports with the sponsor
D. Review the project roadmap for release dates
questions.explanation: Status reports (current and previous) provide the most direct information about where the project currently stands and whether it is on track. They typically include RAG (Red-Amber-Green) status indicators, variance information, and progress metrics. The project Charter documents the initial authorization and agreement from the previous sponsor. The project management plan describes processes and approaches rather than current status. The project roadmap shows planned releases, not current performance.
